title: Implement Montgomery multiplication for GF(p) prime fields
description:
Replace the naive `%`-based arithmetic internals of `Fp

` (350bff7f) with Montgomery multiplication, providing constant-time modular arithmetic without expensive division instructions. ## Background Montgomery multiplication transforms field elements into "Montgomery form" where modular reduction becomes a series of multiplications and bit-shifts, completely eliminating the `DIV`/`REM` instructions that dominate the cost of naive modular arithmetic. This is the standard approach for high-performance prime field implementations. ## Montgomery form An element `a` in GF(p) is stored in Montgomery form as `aR mod P` where `R = 2^64` (for 64-bit limbs). The key insight: reducing `aR * bR` modulo P can be done using only multiplication and addition (the "REDC" algorithm), not division. ## Precomputed constants (const fn) All Montgomery constants are computed at compile time via `const fn`: - `R_MOD_P = 2^64 mod P` — for converting to Montgomery form - `R2_MOD_P = (2^64)^2 mod P = R^2 mod P` — for efficient to_montgomery conversion - `P_INV = -P^{-1} mod 2^64` — the Montgomery reduction constant (mu) ```rust #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Debug)] pub struct Fp { /// Value stored in Montgomery form: self.0 = a * R mod P value: u64, } impl Fp

{ const R_MOD_P: u64 = /* 2^64 mod P, computed by const fn */; const R2_MOD_P: u64 = /* R^2 mod P, computed by const fn */; const P_INV: u64 = /* -P^{-1} mod 2^64, computed by const fn */; } ``` ## REDC algorithm The Montgomery reduction (REDC) for a 128-bit product `t`: ``` fn redc(t: u128) -> u64 { let m = (t as u64).wrapping_mul(P_INV); let t = t + (m as u128) * (P as u128); let result = (t >> 64) as u64; if result >= P { result - P } else { result } } ``` Multiplication: `redc((a.value as u128) * (b.value as u128))` Addition: `(a + b) % P` with branchless correction Subtraction: `(a + P - b) % P` with branchless correction ## API preservation The public API (`Fp

` implementing `FiniteField` + `ConstField`) is **unchanged**. Montgomery form is an internal representation detail: - `Fp::new(val)` converts `val` to Montgomery form internally - `Fp::value()` converts back from Montgomery form for display/comparison - All arithmetic operates in Montgomery form ## Specializations for specific prime shapes Detect special primes at compile time and use faster reduction: - **Mersenne primes** (2^n - 1): Reduction via bitwise AND + ADD (tracked separately in 1f2f8371) - **Proth primes** (k·2^n + 1): Reduction via shifts and subtracts (tracked separately in 1f2f8371) These specializations replace the Montgomery internals for applicable primes but use the same `Fp

` type. ## Location `crates/gf2-core/src/gfp/montgomery.rs` (new file, modifying `Fp

` internals) ## Dependencies - 350bff7f (naive Fp

) — the naive implementation must exist and pass axiom tests first; Montgomery replaces its internals ## Success criteria - All existing `Fp

` axiom tests continue to pass with Montgomery internals. - Cross-verification: naive and Montgomery produce identical results for 10,000+ random operations each across Fp<3>, Fp<65537>, Fp<2^61-1> (30,000+ total random ops via proptest, plus exhaustive coverage for small primes). - Benchmark: naive baselines use runtime-opaque modulus (via `black_box(p)`) to prevent compiler constant-division optimization. Committed results in `crates/gf2-core/benches/BENCHMARK_RESULTS.md` evidence: - >=1.5x speedup over naive `%` for multiplication (both approaches share the widening multiply; Montgomery saves on reduction only) - >=2x speedup over naive `%` for addition/subtraction (branchless correction vs 128-bit division) - >=2x speedup over naive `%` for inversion (compound benefit of Montgomery domain across many squarings) - Constant-time execution: no data-dependent branches in the hot path (multiplication, addition, subtraction).
